---
title: "Week 2: Trust & The Reading Protocol"
subtitle: "Chapters 2–3"
date: "2026-01-27"
format:
  html:
    embed-resources: true
    code-fold: true
    code-tools: true
---

## Overview

This week covers two tightly connected topics: why reproducibility is the foundation of trustworthy research (Chapter 2) and how to read and engage with scholarly work as an active participant rather than a passive consumer (Chapter 3). You'll set up the tools that make reproducible research possible and learn a reading protocol that transforms how you interact with academic articles.

**Reading:** Chapter 2 — The Infrastructure of Trust & Chapter 3 — The Reading Journal Protocol

## Key Concepts

- The replication crisis and why it matters
- Code as documentation (literate programming)
- The spectrum of reproducibility (Levels 0–4)
- Tool introductions: R, RStudio, Quarto, Git/GitHub, Obsidian
- Active vs. passive reading strategies
- IMRAD structure of research articles
- Strategic reading (reverse order)
- Summary vs. synthesis
- Annotation as a skill

## Learning Objectives

By the end of this week, you should be able to:

1. Explain why reproducibility matters and identify the tools that support it
2. Set up your research environment (R, RStudio, Obsidian, Quarto)
3. Read a research article strategically rather than linearly
4. Create a literature note in Obsidian using the course template
5. Distinguish between summarizing and synthesizing sources

## Assignment: Syllabus Contract (25 pts)

### Part 1: Software Verification (15 pts)

1. Install R, RStudio, Obsidian, and Quarto (see course website setup guides)
2. Open the syllabus contract template (provided via Blackboard or coursepackR)
3. Render the template to PDF — this confirms your software is working

### Part 2: Contract Signature (10 pts)

1. Read the syllabus carefully
2. Fill in the acknowledgment fields in the contract template
3. Render to PDF and submit to Blackboard

## Discussion Questions

1. Why might a researcher who gets "correct" results still be doing unreproducible science?
2. When you read a research article, do you start at the beginning? After this week, what would you do differently?
